one_line_takeaway: By fine-tuning a copy of the target LLM with contrastive RL to actively reconstruct candidate suffixes, ADRA/ADRA+ turn membership inference into an active attack that beats passive MIA baselines (Loss, R-Loss, Zlib, Min-K%, Min-K%++, and an n-gram reconstruction baseline) by an average of 10.7% AUROC across pre-training, post-training, and distillation settings, with the largest gain (near-perfect 98.4% AUROC) on detecting DeepSeek-R1 distillation traces.
key_finding: ADRA/ADRA+ consistently and substantially outperform all passive baselines (loss-based and the paper's own N-Sampling reconstruction baseline) across all three training stages, measured by AUROC. Pre-training (Table 3, original/verbatim): ADRA+ reaches 78.4% on BookMIA (Llama2-7B) vs. best passive baseline Loss at 60.6% and N-Sampling at 73.6%; 60.6% on WikiMIA2024 Hard (Qwen2-7B) vs. Min-K%++ 50.6% and N-Sampling 54.3%; 92.4% on Dolma3-arXiv (Olmo3-7B-Instruct) vs. Loss 71.1% and N-Sampling 81.5%. The abstract states ADRA+ improves over Min-K%++ by 10% AUROC on WikiMIA2024 Hard. Post-training (Table 5): ADRA+ reaches 85.9% AUROC on AIME (Tulu2-7B) vs. N-Sampling 72.7% (a 13.2-point gain) and Min-K%++ 78.3% (7.6-point gain, matching the abstract's headline number); 70.5% on Olympia Math vs. N-Sampling 59.5% (11.0-point gain). On Tulu3 Mix, ADRA+ roughly matches or slightly trails R-Loss (which requires an extra calibration model) while beating all calibration-free baselines. Distillation (Table 7): ADRA reaches 98.4% AUROC under DeepSeek-R1-distilled traces (S1.1) vs. N-Sampling 90.4% and Min-K%++ 70.6%; 85.2% under Gemini-2.0-Flash distillation (S1) vs. N-Sampling 80.8% and Min-K%++ 81.7%. Reconstruction quality (Tables 4, 6, 8) also improves with ADRA/ADRA+ over N-Sampling on most lexical (Jaccard, LCS, n-gram coverage) and embedding-cosine metrics, though the paper notes a metric trade-off on WikiMIA2024 Hard where RL increases Jaccard/embedding scores but reduces LCS/coverage. Ablations (Fig. 2, Table 10) show RL-based optimization and the contrastive reward objective are both necessary for gains: naive RL without the contrastive design underperforms, SFT-based fine-tuning actually degrades AUROC as training continues (while RL improves it), and lexical rewards outperform model-based reward alternatives (embedding similarity: 76.9%, LLM-as-judge: 78.8%) which are described as more prone to reward hacking, versus lexical rewards at 81.3% AUROC (Top-5 Mean) on the ablation setting.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Active Data Reconstruction Attack (ADRA), a new family of membership inference attacks (MIAs) against LLMs that, unlike prior passive MIAs (which only query fixed model weights via log-likelihoods or one-shot generations), actively fine-tunes a copy of the target model with on-policy reinforcement learning (GRPO) to elicit reconstruction of a candidate document's suffix from its prefix. The authors design lexical reconstruction reward functions (token-set/Jaccard similarity, longest common subsequence, n-gram set coverage) and a contrastive reward scheme that scores rollouts against a pool containing the true suffix plus sampled distractor suffixes, taking the best match as reward. Two variants are proposed: ADRA (fixed matching against the full pool) and ADRA+ (adaptive matching that uses a Bernoulli switch, driven by a loss-based membership prior such as Min-K%++, to decide whether the true suffix is included in the matching pool for a given rollout). After K RL steps the fine-tuned policy is resampled and reconstruction-similarity scores are aggregated into a membership score. The paper also contributes 6 new MIA benchmark datasets (Dolma3/Olmo3 arXiv mix, Tulu3 Mix on Aya and WildChat, AIME, Olympiad Math, and S1/S1.1 distillation traces) to better match frontier models' later knowledge cutoffs and heavier post-training, alongside evaluation on 2 established benchmarks (BookMIA, WikiMIA2024 Hard).
threat_model: Attacker has full white-box access to a target LLM's weights (able to initialize a policy from them and run on-policy RL fine-tuning), plus a candidate document split into an observed prefix and held-out suffix, plus a pool of distractor candidate documents from which non-member suffixes can be drawn as negatives. The goal is binary membership inference: determine whether a candidate document x was present in the target model's training data (pre-training corpus, post-training/SFT mixture, or distillation traces from a teacher model). This is explicitly framed as a privacy/copyright/data-contamination attack, motivated by concerns about memorization and regurgitation of training data (citing Carlini et al. 2021 extraction attacks and copyright/contamination literature).
limitations: The threat model requires white-box weight access and the compute budget to run on-policy RL fine-tuning per candidate/pool, which is far more expensive than passive log-probability or single-pass generation MIAs and was not compared against baselines on a cost/compute-normalized basis in the excerpted sections. Performance remains modest in the hardest pre-training setting: WikiMIA2024 Hard AUROC tops out at only 60.6% (near chance for a supposedly 'hard' benchmark, the paper's own framing). ADRA+'s adaptive matching relies on a loss-based membership prior (Min-K%/Min-K%++) that is itself an imperfect passive MIA, so ADRA+'s gains are partly bootstrapped from an existing weak signal. In the distillation setting, ADRA+ is explicitly noted as not directly applicable because member and non-member examples share the same prefix (only the suffix differs), so only plain ADRA is used there. R-Loss, a calibration-based passive baseline, still matches or slightly beats ADRA+ on the Tulu3 Mix post-training setting, showing the active RL approach does not dominate universally.

datasets_used: WikiMIA2024 Hard (Hallinan et al. 2025) - pre-training, Qwen2-7B
Dolma3 / Olmo3 Mix arXiv subset (new, this paper) - pre-training, Olmo3-7B-Instruct
AIME 2021-2025 problems (new, this paper) - post-training controlled contamination, Tulu2-7B
Olympiad Math / AI-MO olympiads-ref (new, this paper) - post-training controlled contamination, Tulu2-7B
Tulu3 SFT Mix on Aya and WildChat subsets (new, this paper) - post-training mixture, Tulu3-8B
S1 distillation traces from Gemini-2.0-Flash (new, this paper) - distillation, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct student
S1.1 distillation traces from DeepSeek-R1 (new, this paper) - distillation, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct student
quantitative_results: Pre-training BookMIA (Llama2-7B, original): ADRA+ 78.4% AUROC vs ADRA 76.2%, N-Sampling 73.6%, Loss 60.6%, Zlib 50.4%, Min-K% 63.3%, Min-K%++ 59.6%
Pre-training WikiMIA2024 Hard (Qwen2-7B, original): ADRA+ 60.6% AUROC vs ADRA 59.1%, N-Sampling 54.3%, Loss 45.7%, Min-K% 45.4%, Min-K%++ 50.6%
Pre-training Dolma3 arXiv (Olmo3-7B-Instruct, original): ADRA+ 92.4% AUROC vs ADRA 91.8%, N-Sampling 81.5%, Loss 71.1%, Min-K%++ 37.4%
Post-training AIME (Tulu2-7B, original): ADRA+ 85.9% AUROC vs ADRA 85.6%, N-Sampling 72.7%, R-Loss 67.8%, Min-K%++ 78.3% (abstract-cited 7.6% gain over Min-K%++)
Post-training Olympia Math (Tulu2-7B, original): ADRA+ 70.5% AUROC vs N-Sampling 59.5% (11.0-point gain), R-Loss 70.3%
Post-training Tulu3 Mix (Tulu3-8B): ADRA+ 66.5% AUROC on Aya vs R-Loss 68.0%; 65.6% on WildChat, equal to R-Loss 66.2%
Distillation S1.1 (DeepSeek-R1 traces, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct student): ADRA 98.4% AUROC vs N-Sampling 90.4%, Min-K%++ 70.6%, Loss 55.1%
Distillation S1 (Gemini-2.0-Flash traces): ADRA 85.2% AUROC vs N-Sampling 80.8%, Min-K%++ 81.7%
Tulu2-13B AIME scale-up: ADRA+ 89.5% AUROC vs ADRA 84.4%, N-Sampling 72.7%, Min-K%++ 82.8%
Reward-type ablation (Top-5 Mean AUROC on AIME first seed): Lexical rewards 81.3% vs Embedding-based reward 76.9%, LLM-as-Judge reward 78.8%, No-Training baseline 74.8%

Reconstruction quality, S1.1 distillation: ADRA Lexical Jaccard Best/Avg 45.4/44.5 vs N-Sampling 32.7/25.5; average Jaccard improvement of 19.0 and coverage improvement of 25.4 over N-Sampling under DeepSeek-R1 distillation cited in text
Average improvement of ADRA/ADRA+ over the previous runner-up across all settings: 10.7% (stated in abstract/introduction)
baselines_compared: N-Sampling - this paper's generalization of Hallinan et al. (2025)'s n-gram-coverage reconstruction-based passive MIA, using Average-of-N/Best-of-N aggregation over lexical and embedding similarity metrics
